coredata.dc:title: One-pot green synthesis of oxygen-rich nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and their potential application in pH-sensitive photoluminescence and detection of mercury(II) ions
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Bingfang
Zhang, Liangliang
Lan, Chuanqing
Zhao, Jingjin
Su, Yubin
Zhao, Shulin
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen doping has been a powerful method to modulate the properties of carbon materials for various applications, and N-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gained remarkable interest because of their unique chemical, electronic, and optical properties. Herein, we introduce a facile one-pot solid-phase synthesis strategy for N-doped GQDs using citric acid (CA) as the carbon source and 3,4-dihydroxy-l -phenylalanine (l-DOPA) as the N source. The as-prepared N-GQDs with oxygen-rich functional groups are uniform with an average diameter of 12.5nm. Because of the introduction of nitrogen atoms, N-GQDs exhibit excitation-wavelength-independent fluorescence with the maximum emission at 445nm, and a high quantum yield of 18% is achieved at an excitation wavelength of 346nm. Furthermore, a highly efficient fluorosensor based on the as-prepared N-GQDs was developed for the detection of Hg2+ because of the effective quenching effect of metal ions via nonradiative electron transfer. This fluorosensor exhibits high sensitivity toward Hg2+ with a detection limit of 8.6nM. The selectivity experiments reveal that the fluorescent sensor is specific for Hg2+. Most importantly, the practical use of the sensor based on N-GQDs for Hg2+ detection was successfully demonstrated in river-water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxygen-rich nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence probe
Green synthesis
pH-Sensitive photoluminescence
Mercury

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real sample
Fluorescence quenching of Hg2+ to the N-OGQDs
Optimization of conditions for Hg2+ detection
Sensitivity of the N-OGQDs for Hg2+ detection
Selectivity of N-OGQDs for Hg2+ detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of the as-prepared N-OGQDs (a) and HRTEM image of N-OGQD (b).
XRD (a) and Raman spectra (b) of the as-prepared N-OGQDs.
XPS (a), N 1s (b), C 1s (c) and O 1s spectra (d) of the as-prepared N-OGQDs.
FTIR spectra of GQDs (trace a) and as-prepared N-OGQDs (trace b).

The optical properties of the as-prepared N-OGQDs. (a) UV and fluorescence spectra of the N-OGQDs. Inset: photographs taken under daylight (left) and 346nm UV light (right); and (b) fluorescence emission spectra of the as-prepared N-OGQDs at different excitation wavelengths.

pH-Dependent PL spectra the as-prepared N-OGQDs when the pH is switched between 3 and 8. Fluorescence spectra of the N-OGQDs in different pH values of Tris–HCl buffer (10mM) solution with excitation at 346nm (a), and a linear relationship between PL intensity of the N-OGQDs and solution pH value (b).

Fluorescence emission spectra of the as-prepared N-OGQDs in aqueous solution upon addition of various concentrations of Hg2+ (from top to bottom: 0, 0.04, 0.08, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 4.0 and 8.0μM), excitation at 346nm (a), and the dependence of the fluorescence quenching efficiency on the concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 0–8.0μM; inset shows the linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency and Hg2+ concentration (b).

Fluorescence intensity of the N-OGQDs in the presence and absence of different metal ions (3μM) listed from left to right: blank, Hg2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ba2+ and Ag+.

Fluorescence response of N-OGQDs–Hg2+ mixture in the presence of TEA (50mM, green bar) and SHPP (10mM, blue bar) to Fe3+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ni2+ and Cu2+ ions. The concentrations of Fe3+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ni2+ and Cu2+ are 100, 3.0, 3.0 and 3.0mM, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Possible formation process of the N-OGQDs and the sensing principle of the N-OGQDs based fluorescent probe for Hg2+.
: Comparison of different nanoparticle-based fluorescent probes for Hg2+ detection.

head.ce:title: One-pot green synthesis of oxygen-rich nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and their potential application in pH-sensitive photoluminescence and detection of mercury(II) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Pharmacy, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Pharmacy, Guangxi Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Life Sciences, Baise University, Baise, Guangxi 533000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Life Sciences, Baise University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen doping has been a powerful method to modulate the properties of carbon materials for various applications, and N-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gained remarkable interest because of their unique chemical, electronic, and optical properties. Herein, we introduce a facile one-pot solid-phase synthesis strategy for N-doped GQDs using citric acid (CA) as the carbon source and 3,4-dihydroxy-l -phenylalanine (l-DOPA) as the N source. The as-prepared N-GQDs with oxygen-rich functional groups are uniform with an average diameter of 12.5nm. Because of the introduction of nitrogen atoms, N-GQDs exhibit excitation-wavelength-independent fluorescence with the maximum emission at 445nm, and a high quantum yield of 18% is achieved at an excitation wavelength of 346nm. Furthermore, a highly efficient fluorosensor based on the as-prepared N-GQDs was developed for the detection of Hg2+ because of the effective quenching effect of metal ions via nonradiative electron transfer. This fluorosensor exhibits high sensitivity toward Hg2+ with a detection limit of 8.6nM. The selectivity experiments reveal that the fluorescent sensor is specific for Hg2+. Most importantly, the practical use of the sensor based on N-GQDs for Hg2+ detection was successfully demonstrated in river-water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of new types of photosensitive nanomaterials, including semiconductor quantum dots, metal nanorods, and metal nanoclusters, has attracted increasing attention because they provide opportunities for the fabrication and design of new devices with extraordinary properties and functions used especially in biomedical imaging, diagnostics and sensing [1]. However, concerns over the cytotoxicity of heavy-metal constituents and conflicting results from in vitro and animal-toxicity studies have limited their transition towards clinical applications [2,3]. Recently, there has been a splurge of interest in fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials, which are highly regarded in the fluorescent nanomaterial family because of their unique properties, such as low toxicity, high luminescence, robust chemical inertness and easy preparation; these unique properties make fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials advantageous over heavy-metal-based quantum dots and the dye-based fluorescence probes are used for sensing and imaging [4]. A variety of biosensors designed from carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials has been employed to detect nucleic acids, proteins, toxins and small molecules [5–8].

More recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gained remarkable interest as a developed popular type of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials because of their unique chemical, electronic and optical properties [9]. Consequently, GQDs are widely considered as great promising for applications in sensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, photovoltaic devices, and photocatalysis due to their low cytotoxicity, excellent solubility, good biocompatibility, and stable photoluminescence (PL) [10–12]. Inspired by the significant advantages and successful applications of GQDs, several intriguing strategies have been developed to prepare GQDs [13–15]. However, the quantum yields of the as-synthesized GQDs in previous efforts are much lower than those of conventional semiconductor quantum dots. Accordingly, there are still gaps in knowledge regarding the efficient improvement of the quantum yield of GQDs.

Thus far, several alternative strategies have been developed to improve the quantum yield of the GQDs [16,17]. The reported strategies indicate that the intrinsic properties of GQDs can be effectively tuned by doping with heteroatoms and/or surface passivation [18]. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have received significant attention because doping chemically-bonded N atoms manipulates their chemical reactivity and electronic properties [19]. By taking advantages of their unique chemical and optical properties, N-GQDs and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CODs) have been widely employed in various fields such as fuel cells [20], electrocatalysis [21], biosensing [22], and the detection of heavy-metal ions and small molecules [23,24]. The currently, available synthesis approaches employed to fabricate N-GQDs include electrochemical [1], organic-synthesis [25], and hydrothermal methods [26]. Although several approaches have been identified to be successful for synthesizing N-GQDs for the desired possible applications, the N doping of GQDs inevitably requires harsh reaction conditions such as bias voltages, high temperature, high pressure, and multi-step synthesis. Moreover, these methods are complex, time consuming and expensive, which limits their widespread applications. Recently, much advancement has been made in the development of strategies to fabricate self-passivated N-GQDs through high-temperature or microwave-assisted hydrothermal carbonization [22–24]. However, the fluorescence quantum yield of as-synthesized N-GQDs has not been enhanced as much as anticipated. In particular, little is known about how to control the distribution and bonding configurations of the nitrogen dopants or about the doping effects and ill-controlled material properties. In addition, developing novel applications for obtaining fluorescent N-GQDs is still a challenge.

The pH is of great concern to all life forms. Excessive human activities and untreated sewage cause changes in the environmental pH and the acidification of soils, streams, lakes and seawater, which in turn pollute drinking water [4]. Thus, monitoring the pH level of the environment is crucial for environment protection.

The accumulation of heavy-metal ions in the environment has generated considerable concern because of their strong toxicity, bioaccumulation and risk of damage to the humans even at low concentrations. Hg2+ may cause toxicological effects ranking from various cognitive and motion disorders to kidney failure and brain damage [27]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop a simple, sensitive, and selective assay for determining Hg2+ concentrations.

Herein, we introduce a facile one-pot solid-phase synthesis strategy to obtain a high quantum yield of oxygen-rich N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-OGQDs) using citric acid (CA) as the carbon source and 3,4-dihydroxy-l-phenylalanine (l-DOPA) as the nitrogen source. The synthesized N-OGQDs have a relatively uniform size and surface state and exhibit excitation-wavelength-independent fluorescence with a high quantum yield of approximately 18.0% at the strongest emission around 445nm. The process for the formation of the N-OGQDs does not require special equipment, and the experimental conditions are easily controlled through temperature and time regulation, implying that the proposed process proposed is relatively simple and environment-friendly. Most importantly, for the presence of oxygen-rich and nitrous groups on the surface of the obtained N-OGQDs, the sensing system based on N-OGQDs can be used for directly measuring the pH (from 3 to 8) of a solution based on the protonation of the functional groups on the N-OGQDs surface, and for the label-free detection of low concentrations of Hg2+ ions based on the strong affinity of Hg2+ ions to the O and N atoms on the surface of N-OGQDs ( Scheme 1). We further demonstrate that the sensing system based on N-OGQDs can serve as an effective fluorescent platform for the sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ with a limit of detection of 8.6nM, which is much lower than those of many previously reported fluorescent sensors for Hg2+ detection. Moreover, the practical use of this sensor for the determination of Hg2+ in real water samples was also demonstrated.

CA, l-DOPA, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, ZnCl2, CaCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, AgNO3 and Pb(NO3)2 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tris, NaOH, HCl, triethanolamine (TEA) and sodium hexametaphoshpate (SHHP) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. Water was purified by employing a Milli-Q plus 185 equip from Millipore (Bedford, MA) and used throughout the work.

CA (1.0g) and l -DOPA (1.0g) were placed in a 25mL cuvette and heated to 230°C using a heating mantle under vigorous stirring. Approximately 40min later (the size of N-OGQDs increases with the increase of reaction time), the liquid changed from colorless to pale yellow and then orange. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the liquid was ultrasonicated and dissolved in 10mL water. The pH of the orange liquid for preparing N-OGQDs was adjusted to 7.0 by adding NaOH under stirring. The obtained mixture was dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 72h (molecular-weight cutoff of 1000, Shanghai Green Bird Science & Technology Development Co., China) to remove impurities and excess l-DOPA, yielding a brown solution. The solvent was removed using a rotary evaporator. Then the obtained N-OGQD was dispersed in water and preserved at 4°C for further characterization and use.

The fluorescence emission spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra of the N-OGQDs were recorded using a Perkin-Elmer LS-55 luminescence spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer Instruments, USA) and a Cary 60 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies) at room temperature, respectively. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) study was performed using KBr pellets on a PerkinElmer FTIR spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was measured by an FEI Tecnai F-20 field-emission TEM (Hillsboro, OR, USA) operating at 200kV. The samples for TEM measurements were prepared through the deposition of one drop of a water dispersion of the sample onto carbon-coated copper TEM grids using pipettes, and the solvent was removed under ambient conditions. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using a Kratos XSAM 800 photoelectron spectrometer (Manchester, UK), where the analysis chamber was 1.5×10−9 mbar and the X-ray spot was 500μm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed using a RigakuD/max 2500v/pc X-ray powder diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation (λ=0.154nm). Fluorescence lifetime experiments were conducted on a time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer FL3-P-TCSC (Horiba Jobin Jvon, France).

Fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) was determined by using quinine sulfate (Φ=0.54 in 0.05M H2SO4) as a standard [28]. The quantum yield was calculated according to the following equation:
Φ
x
=
Φ
s
×
A
s
A
x
×
F
x
F
s
×
n
x
2
n
s
2
where Φ is the quantum yield, F is the measured integrated emission intensity, A is the optical density, and n is the refractive index of the solvent (1.33 for water). The subscript “s” refers to the reference with a known quantum yield, and “x” refers to the as-prepared N-OGQDs.

The fluorescence sensing of Hg2+ was performed in a Tris–HCl buffer solution (10mM, pH 8.0) at room temperature. In a typical run, an aliquot of N-OGQDs (0.14mg/mL, 10μL) was added to 80μL of Tris–HCl buffer, following which Hg2+ were added with various concentrations. The solution was mixed thoroughly and left at room temperature for 10min for the reaction to complete. Subsequently, the fluorescence quenching spectra were recorded. To evaluate the selectivity towards Hg2+, different concentrations of metal ions other than Hg2+ were added into the aqueous solution in a similar manner. The fluorescence quenching spectra were recorded under the following conditions: excitation wavelength of 346nm; maximum emission at 445nm; slit widths of 15.0 and 15.0nm for excitation and emission, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the N-OGQDs-based sensor for the detection of Hg2+ in a real sample was verified using river-water samples obtained from Lijiang river in Guilin, China. The river water was first filtered through a 0.45μm membrane and then centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 20min to remove any suspended particles. A series of samples was prepared by adding TEA and SHHP for few minutes, and then spiking standard solutions containing various concentrations of Hg2+ (from 0.2 to 3.0μM) in 80μL Tris–HCl buffer (10mM, pH 8.0) into the river water. The obtained solutions were further mixed with N-OGQDs solutions (0.14mg/mL, 10μL), and then the fluorescence spectra were recorded (excitation wavelength: 346nm).

The morphology of the N-OGQDs was characterized using TEM. The TEM image in Fig. 1a shows that the prepared N-OGQDs are highly dispersed. The sizes were mainly in the range of 4–25nm (12.5nm average), as shown in Fig. 1b. The size distribution of prepared N-OGQDs is close to that of reported GQD in literature [10]. Although the range of size distribution is relatively wide, almost 74% of N-OGQDs is concentrated in 10–15nm. The process of l-DOPA functionalized N-OGQDs is necessary to minimize heterogeneity and avoid cross-reaction over the experimental format [29,30].

The XRD pattern of the N-OGQDs ( Fig. 2a) shows a wide (002) peak centered at 25° (around 0.34nm), which is consistent with the lattice spacing of graphite [31]. It is suggested that CA was carbonized to produce graphite structures, which is consistent with the TEM results. The Raman spectrum of the N-OGQDs (Fig. 2b) was recorded to investigate the microstructure of the N-OGQDs further. The Raman spectrum shows a disordered (D) band at approximately 1360cm−1 and a crystalline (G) band at 1575cm−1, which are related to the presence of sp3 defects and in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon, respectively. The ratio of intensities of D and G bands (I D/I G) is approximately 0.77 for the N-OGQDs, indicating that the as-prepared N-OGQDs have a structure similar to that of graphite and a highly crystalline nature [32], which is in accordance with the TEM results. The results of TEM, XRD, and Raman analyses confirm that the graphene framework formed at this stage results from the incompletely carbonized CA.

XPS measurement was performed to investigate the composition of the N-OGQDs. The XPS results of N-OGQD are shown in Fig. 3, in which C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s signals can be seen at 284.4, 399.2, and 532.7eV, respectively (Fig. 3a). In the high-resolution N 1s XPS measurement of N-OGQDs (Fig. 3b), a clear fitted peak is observed at 400.1eV with a small peak at 401.1eV; these peaks correspond to the pyrrolic N and graphite N, respectively [33]. The existence of pyrrolic N indicates that N bonds with C to form 5 member-ring structures, which are formed from dehydrolysis between the neighbor carboxyl and amide of DOPA. The weak N 1s signal at 401.1eV indicates that only trace amounts of N can enter the graphene framework. Elemental analysis results show that the nitrogen content is 4.01%, which is consistent with the fact that the degree of N doping in the as-prepared N-OGQDs layer is relatively low. As can be seen from Fig. 3c, the C 1s XPS spectra can be fitted into 4 peaks at 284.5, 285.2, 286.01, and 288.9eV, which are assigned to the sp2 C (C–C or C=C) in graphene at 284.5eV, sp3 C from C–N and C–O at 285.2eV, C=O or C=N at 286.01eV, and C(O)–O at 288.9eV in the carboxyl group [34]. The O 1s spectrum of the N-OGQDs can be deconvoluted into three peaks, which correspond to C–OH at 531.1eV, C=O at 531.8eV, and C–O–C at 532.9eV (Fig. 3d) [5]. The results of elemental analysis demonstrate that the composition of the as-prepared N-OGQDs is 66.58wt% C, 4.01wt% N, 5.77wt% H, and 23.64wt% O (calculated), which are in good agreement with the XPS test.

FTIR was used to characterize the functional groups in the prepared N-OGQDs. As shown in Fig. 4, in the spectra of both N-OGQDs and GQDs, the absorption bands at 3245 and 1076cm−1 are assigned to O–H and C–O stretching-vibration peaks, respectively. These peaks suggest that both N-OGQDs and GQDs contain –OH and –COOH groups, which improve the stability and hydrophilicity of the N-OGQDs in aqueous solution. However, the peak at 1578cm−1 in the spectrum of GQDs disappears in the spectrum of N-OGQDs, suggesting the consumption of –COOH groups due to formation of amide in the N-OGQDs. In addition, new doublet peaks at 1622cm−1 and 1450cm−1, corresponding to N–H bending and C–N stretching, can be observed in the spectrum of N-OGQDs, which is caused by the successful incorporation of nitrogen atoms into the N-OGQDs layer through the present synthesis process [35]. These results again confirm that nitrogen atoms have not only been successfully doped into the N-OGQDs layer but also functionalized the obtained N-OGQDs through the present synthesis process [36].

To explore the optical properties of the N-OGQDs, the UV–vis absorption and excitation-dependent PL behavior of the N-OGQDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5a, there are two clear absorption bands at 280nm and 346nm with a weak absorption band at 230nm. It is well known that the two absorption peaks at approximately 230nm and 280nm are often exhibited in the UV–vis spectrum of undoped GQDs and are related to the n→π* and π→π* transitions of C=C and C=O bonds, respectively [15]. Interestingly, N doping introduces a new UV absorption peak at 346nm. We also observed a strong blue emission band of the as-prepared N-OGQD at approximately 445nm and that the change in excitation wavelength from 330nm to 380nm does not cause a notable shift (Fig. 5b). It is suggested that both the size and surface state of the as-prepared N-OGQDs are uniform [37]. In addition, the decay curves of the as-prepared N-OGQDs fitted well to single-exponential functions with lifetimes of 0.96ns. Such a short lifetime indicates that the luminescence mechanism is possibly the radioactive recombination of excited carriers [38]. This single-exponential decay of N-OGQDs is consistent with the property of excitation-independent emission (Fig. 5b), suggesting that the fluorescence originates from a single species [35]. Besides, the quantum yield of the N-OGQDs calculated using quinine sulfate as a reference is up to 18.0%, which is higher than the reported quantum yield of CQDs (15.7%) [22], GQDs (11.4%) [39], and N-GQDs (7.5%) [40] prepared through other methods. The existence of nitrogen- and oxygen-rich functional groups, which are generally excellent auxochromes, could be the reason for the significant enhancement in PL properties.

The emission spectra of the as-prepared N-OGQD solution were recorded under continuous excitation at 365nm with a Xe lamp for 30min. As shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting information), the fluorescence intensity of N-OGQDs shows little change. To confirm the stability of N-OGQDs under high-ionic-strength environments, their fluorescence intensities were also measured in the presence of different concentrations of NaCl (up to 1.0M). As shown in Fig. S2 (Supporting information), only a slight change in the fluorescence intensity is observed, indicating the high stability of N-OGQDs even under a high-ionic-strength environment. This finding suggests that N-OGQDs have great potential for sensing applications under physiological conditions.

The as-prepared N-OGQDs are pH-sensitive with a broad response region. As shown in Fig. 6, an increase in the fluorescence intensity of the N-OGQD solution can be observed with an increase in the solution pH under 346-nm UV light (Fig. 6a), and there is a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the N-OGQDs and solution pH within the range 3–8 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9930 (Fig. 6b). Based on the above results, it can be deduced that the protonation state of the N-OGQDs plays an important role in the fluorescence intensity [41]. For an increase in solution pH from 3 to 8, the deprotonation degree of the N-OGQDs gradually increases, producing higher concentrations of deprotonated nitrogen and oxygen atoms on the surface of the N-OGQDs, which change the electron distributions. In addition, the variation in fluorescence intensity of the N-OGQDs may be attributed to the protonation and deprotonation of amide and carboxylic functional groups on the N-OGQD surface. The above results show that the N-OGQDs have potential as fluorescent sensors for pH measurement, especially in the physiological pH range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of Hg2+ to the N-OGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the feasibility of using the N-OGQDs for detection of Hg2+, the fluorescence spectra of the N-OGQDs under different conditions were investigated. As shown in Fig. S3 (Supporting information), the N-OGQDs exhibit a strong fluorescence peak at 445nm without the addition of Hg2+ (Fig. S3, black curve). Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of the N-OGQDs decreased upon increasing the Hg2+ concentration, which indicates that Hg2+ can effectively quench the fluorescence of the N-OGQDs (Fig. S3, red and green curves). Fluorescence lifetime measurements were conducted to demonstrate the mechanism of quenching in the fluorescence of N-OGQDs due to Hg2+. From two-exponential law of the fluorescence decay curves, the fluorescence lifetime of the original N-OGQDs decreased from 0.957ns (τ 1=0.910ns, τ 2=0.047ns) to 0.696ns (τ 1=0.684ns, τ 2=0.012ns) in the presence of Hg2+ at 0.5μM (Fig. S4, Supporting information). Therefore, it can be concluded that the quenching was dynamic in nature. Based on previous reports that the lifetime of semiconductor colloids decreases after an electron acceptor or a surface modifier adsorbs onto the semiconductor surface [42], the quenching can be predicted to be caused by the electron-transfer mechanism [15]. Fluorescent lifetime of 0.96ns for the as-prepared N-OGQDs is shorter than CdTe/CdS core/shell quantum dots [43]. Such a short fluorescent lifetime of 0.96ns can perform single and multiple-form Förster resonance energy transfer between the N-OGQDs and quencher, and it is possibly caused by QD to act as a donor sensitizing Hg2+ [44,45]. In order to obtain further insight into the mechanism of quenching, UV–vis absorption spectra were used to verify this prediction. As shown in Fig. S5 (Supporting information), the absorption threshold of the N-OGQDs gradually shifted to a shorter wavelength to some extent in the presence of 2 and 5μM Hg2+, which suggests that this shift is attributable to the formation of stable metal complexes due to the strong chemical bonding of Hg2+ to O atoms on the surface of N-OGQDs. On the basis of the above observations, it is concluded that the stronger affinity of Hg2+ with O atoms of the N-OGQDs brings them in close proximity with each other [46]. Therefore, nonradiative electron transfer occurs from the excited states of N-OGQDs to the d orbital of Hg2+, leading to fluorescence quenching [7].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of conditions for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of pH on the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the response of N-OGQDs to Hg2+ were investigated in the Tris–HCl buffer (10mM) with different pH values. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of the response of N-OGQDs to 2.0μM Hg2+ at various pH values is shown in Fig. S6 (Supporting information). It can be seen that an increase in the pH value from 6.5 to 8.0 leads to a remarkable increase in the fluorescence quenching efficiency of N-OGQDs, but a further increase to 9.0 results in a gradual decrease of the fluorescence quenching efficiency. Accordingly, a Tris–HCl buffer (10mM) of pH 8.0 was chosen as the buffer with the optimum pH for Hg2+ detection. The time-dependent fluorescence spectra of the N-OGQDs–Hg2+ solution (Fig. S7, Supporting information) suggest that only 10min is required to complete the reaction between the N-OGQDs and Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the N-OGQDs for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: For the sensitivity study, changes in fluorescence intensity of the N-OGQDs based on the “turn off” mechanism were measured after the addition of different concentrations of Hg2+ for a fixed time of 10min. Fig. 7a shows the change in fluorescence intensity of the N-OGQDs upon adding different concentrations of Hg2+ into the sensing system based on N-OGQDs under optimized conditions. As expected, the fluorescence intensity of the N-OGQDs at 445nm decreased with increase in Hg2+ concentration, revealing that the sensor based on N-OGQDs is sensitive to Hg2+. Fig. 7b outlines the relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the N-OGQDs and the Hg2+ concentration. The figure indicates that the fluorescence quenching efficiency increased with increasing concentration of Hg2+ ions and the value of fluorescence quenching efficiency reached a saturation point when the Hg2+ concentration reached 6μM. The inset of Fig. 7b shows good linearity between the fluorescence quenching efficiency and concentration of Hg2+ ions. The regression relation is y=0.01798+0.2168x–0.03399x 2+0.00182x 3 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9981 for Hg2+ concentrations ranging from 0.04 to 6μM, where y and x represent the fluorescence quenching efficiency and Hg2+ concentration, respectively. The detection limit is estimated to be 8.6nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, which is much lower than many previously reported detection limits with different fluorescence probes ( Table 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of N-OGQDs for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the specificity of the sensing system based on N-OGQDs for Hg2+ detection, the changes in fluorescence intensity were examined in the presence of competitive metal ions under the optimal conditions. Fig. 8 shows the fluorescence quenching result of the N-OGQDs in the presence of Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, and Ag+. It is noteworthy that the fluorescence intensity of the N-OGQDs is remarkably decreased in the presence of Hg2+. However, no obvious decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed with the addition of Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, or Ag+ into the solution of N-OGQDs. Unexpectedly, the fluorescence intensity of the N-OGQDs decreases clearly upon the addition of Pb2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, or Ni2+. These observations suggest that Pb2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, and Ni2+ may strongly influence Hg2+ detection performance. The selectivity of the N-OGQDs to Hg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, and Ni2+ is higher because these ions have a higher thermodynamic affinity and faster chelating process with the N and O atoms of the surface of N-OGQDs in comparison with the other metal ions considered. In addition, it can be observed that the presence of Fe3+ at a high concentration also results in a dramatic decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the N-OGQDs, indicating that Fe3+ with high concentration also influences Hg2+ detection. Fortunately, the issue mentioned above can be circumvented by using triethanolamine (TEA) and sodium hexametaphoshpate (SHPP) as chelating agents for Pb2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, and Fe3+. Indeed, the coexistence of Fe3+ with the N-OGQDs–Hg2+ mixture in the presence of SHPP does not affect Hg2+ detection. On the other hand, the addition of Pb2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, and Ni2+ into the above sensing system in the presence of TEA does not influence Hg2+ detection ( Fig. 9). Thus, it can be concluded that the addition of TEA or SHHP in the N-OGQDs–Hg2+ sensing system can successfully mask Fe3+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, and Ni2+ ions, which allow the recovery of the selective detection of Hg2+. Therefore, the present fluorescence nanoprobes are suitable to detect Hg2+ with high sensitivity and selectivity.

Mercury species in the aquatic environment include elemental mercury (Hg0, 10–30% of total [Hg]) and the oxidation states (Hg+ and Hg2+, 70–90% of total [Hg]) [53,54]. The developed sensing system based on N-OGQDs was applied for the detection of Hg2+ ions in Lijiang river to confirm its applicability in analyzing real samples. The real water samples were spiked with standard solutions with different concentrations of Hg2+. As can be seen from Fig. S8 (Supporting information), the fluorescence intensity decreases with the increase in Hg2+ concentration, and the fluorescence quenching efficiency exhibits a linear relationship with the concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 0.2–3.0μM. The assay results are given in Table S1 (Supporting information); the recoveries were found to be in the range of 86.7–105.3%. The assay precision was examined by repeatedly analyzing each serum sample six times within a working day. The relative standard deviation (RSD, n=6) was less than 4%. Despite the possible interference from the numerous organics and minerals existing in river water, this sensing system can satisfactorily detect Hg2+. These results indicate that the proposed method has excellent applicability for Hg2+ detection in real samples.

In summary, stable and water-soluble fluorescent N-OGQDs were synthesized using an environment-friendly and one-step solid-phase method with CA as the carbon source and l -DOPA as the N source. Furthermore, the method requires no special equipment for the formation of N-OGQDs or any further chemical modification of N-OGQDs. Therefore, the method is simple and inexpensive. The obtained N-OGQDs exhibit excitation-wavelength-independent fluorescence with a high quantum yield of 18.0%. N-OGQDs have been further chosen as the fluorescence probe for the label-free detection of Hg2+. By taking advantage of the high content of O and N atoms on the surface of the N-OGQDs, the sensing system based on N-OGQDs directly detects Hg2+ with a detection limit of 8.6nM, which is superior to most existing approaches for Hg2+ ion analysis. The practical application of this probe for Hg2+ detection in real water samples was demonstrated. Therefore, the easily synthesized fluorescent N-OGQD-based probe may have significant potential applications in environmental protection for Hg2+ detection in environmental water samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21175030; 21305020), and BAGUI Scholar Program as well as Science Foundations of Guangxi Normal University (2013ZD006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2015.04.059.
